text.title: Controller Unit Test Patterns
text.summary: xUnit unit test structure for ASP.NET Core controllers using Moq and Arrange-Act-Assert.

rule.statement: Use the constructor for all test setup; never use [Fact]-level setup methods. xUnit creates a new instance per test, so the constructor IS the setup.
rule.rationale: Shared state between tests causes flaky, order-dependent failures that are hard to diagnose.

section.title: Fact tests: happy path and not-found

rule.statement: Write one [Fact] per branch (happy path, not-found, validation failure). Assert on result type first, then content.
rule.rationale: One assertion concept per test makes failure messages unambiguous and avoids hiding secondary failures.

rule.statement: Use [Theory] + [InlineData] or [MemberData] for data-driven branches. Deduplicate InlineData rows — identical logical cases waste coverage signal.
rule.rationale: Theory collapses N similar test bodies into one parameterized test, reducing maintenance and making gaps visible.

antipattern.description: Over-specifying Verify calls on implementation-detail interactions.
antipattern.reason: Moq.Verify on internal calls couples tests to implementation; refactoring breaks tests even when behavior is correct.

antipattern.description: Stubbing extension methods or non-virtual members with Moq.
antipattern.reason: Moq generates "Expression tree may not contain..." errors. Wrap in an interface instead.
Produces useless failure output. Use Assert.Equal(expected, actual) or FluentAssertions .Should().Be().

text.reference: Full controller test skeleton with Moq setup and result assertions.
